one_line_takeaway: A broad narrative survey mapping RAG-specific attack surfaces (data poisoning, membership inference, adversarial perturbation, embedding inversion, indirect prompt injection) to input-side and output-side defenses and to evaluation benchmarks/metrics across roughly 152 reviewed papers, concluding RAG security is fundamentally limited by a retrieval-driven 'trust paradox,' an embedding 'semantic gap,' and an unresolved privacy-efficiency tradeoff in current encryption schemes.
key_finding: The survey's own synthesized conclusions (section 6, Table 6) are: (1) RAG's structural dependence on external retrieved content creates a 'Trust Paradox' / 'Contextual Security Gap' -- even safety-aligned models suppress internal safety priors and defer to retrieved context when it is malicious or misleading, and the survey cites that attacks such as CorruptRAG and PoisonedRAG can induce predefined false or malicious outputs 'with a success rate often exceeding 90%' by injecting a minimal amount of poisoned text into a large corpus (citation [120], not the survey's own experiment); (2) a 'Semantic Gap' between human language and dense vector representations makes embedding-space adversarial collisions an intrinsic, currently irreducible weakness of retrieval systems that rely solely on cosine-similarity-based dense retrieval without a symbolic verification layer; (3) current privacy defenses (FHE, TEE, plaintext retrieval) are trapped in a 'Privacy-Efficiency zero-sum game' -- fully homomorphic encryption is too slow for real-time retrieval, TEEs have side-channel risk, and plaintext retrieval turns RAG into a 'data leakage amplifier' vulnerable to logit-analysis and embedding-inversion attacks. The survey's descriptive literature statistics (Fig. 1) report RAG-security publication counts of 3 (2022), 4 (2023), 18 (2024), and 48 (2025) papers, and a topic-share breakdown where Data Poisoning Attacks constitute 28.4% of surveyed literature, Defense Frameworks 14.9%, Privacy Preservation 10.4%, Membership Inference Attacks 10.4%, Reliability and Security 7.5%, Benchmarks 7.5%, Adversarial Attacks 7.5%, Data and Model Protection 6%, Cryptographic Defenses 3%, Embedding Inversion Attacks 3%, and Indirect Attacks 1.5%.
core_contribution: A systematic literature survey (stated to be based on analysis of 152 papers) of security threats, defenses, and evaluation resources for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) systems. The authors claim it is the first end-to-end survey of RAG security that maps the entire pipeline (vector-database construction, retriever, generator) rather than isolating a single vulnerability class, as prior surveys (Wu 2025, Gu 2025, He 2025, Arz 2025, Wang 2025) do per the paper's own comparison table (Table 1). The paper's own contributions are: (1) a taxonomy (Fig. 2) organizing RAG threats into data poisoning, membership inference, adversarial, and 'other' (embedding inversion, indirect prompt injection/jailbreak) attacks, each mapped to the specific pipeline stage/module it targets; (2) a taxonomy of input-side defenses (access control, data cleansing/filtering, encryption including homomorphic encryption) and output-side defenses (differential privacy, federated learning, entity masking) (Fig. 8, Fig. 9, Fig. 10); (3) a compiled catalogue and comparison of RAG security evaluation datasets/benchmarks (Table 5) and metric systems (ASR, Retrieval Accuracy, F1(correct)/F1(incorrect), Attack Failure Rate, Detection Accuracy/FPR/FNR, Expected Exposure, Attribution Weight); (4) a synthesized set of three 'insights' (Trust Paradox / Contextual Security Gap, Semantic Gap, Privacy-Efficiency zero-sum game), three challenges, and three future-research prospects (Table 6, section 6). The survey does not propose or evaluate any new attack, defense, or benchmark itself -- all reported attack success rates, model names, and dataset statistics are drawn from and attributed to the cited primary-research papers it reviews (e.g., PoisonedRAG, PR-Attack, BRRA, SafeRAG, DP-RAG).
threat_model: Not the paper's own -- this is a review paper with no original threat model. It aggregates the threat models used by the surveyed literature: (1) knowledge-base/data-poisoning attackers who inject crafted text into the external corpus so it is both retrieved (high embedding similarity to a target query) and persuasive enough to override the generator's prior knowledge (attack requires satisfying a 'retrieval condition' and a 'generation condition' simultaneously, per the paper's Algorithm 1); (2) membership-inference attackers who query a deployed RAG system with candidate documents/entities and use output confidence, perplexity, or semantic similarity to infer whether a document exists in the private knowledge base (Algorithm 2); (3) adversarial-perturbation attackers who optimize discrete text perturbations under a joint retrieval-reward/generation-reward/stealthiness loss to manipulate retrieval ranking or induce harmful generation while remaining fluent (Algorithm 3); (4) embedding-inversion attackers who reconstruct original text from intercepted embedding vectors without model weight access; (5) indirect prompt-injection/jailbreak attackers who plant instructions in documents that get retrieved as 'trusted context' and hijack the LLM's control flow because RAG systems cannot distinguish system instructions from retrieved content.
limitations: As acknowledged in the survey's own outlook/conclusion sections: (1) existing RAG defenses lack formal security proofs; (2) evaluation benchmarks are fragmented, static, and lag behind adaptive/stealthy poisoning attacks, with security metrics highly coupled to retrieval-quality/generation-faithfulness metrics, and automated 'LLM-as-a-Judge' evaluation is noted (citation [127]) to suffer from bias and hallucination; (3) real-time/runtime defenses for encrypted vector operations remain immature -- current encryption is mostly limited to static storage and does not protect high-frequency runtime similarity search; (4) Agentic RAG introduces new unaddressed risks (cross-agent poisoning propagation, uncontrolled tool invocation) that current text-matching-based defenses cannot semantically screen. Independent of the authors' own stated limitations: the survey does not describe an explicit, reproducible search/inclusion methodology (no PRISMA diagram, search-query terms, or database list given in the paper) for how the 152 papers were selected, so its coverage claims cannot be independently audited from the paper text; and, being a review, all quantitative attack/defense numbers are second-hand (attributed to cited primary papers), so their reliability depends entirely on those original studies.

datasets_used: HotpotQA (113K QA pairs, 10 topics, 2018) -- cataloged in the survey's Table 5 as a multi-hop QA benchmark used by cited RAG-security papers, not run by the survey authors
Natural Questions / NQ (307K train, 15K test pairs, 2019) -- cataloged single-hop QA benchmark
TREC 2022 Fair Ranking Track corpus (6.47M+ docs, 48 queries, 2022) -- used by cited work [116] for 8-category cognitive-template fairness evaluation (368 derived informational queries)
AdvBench (520 harmful instruction samples, 2023) -- cataloged generic LLM adversarial benchmark, no RAG-specific attack design per the survey's own limitation note
AlpacaEval (529 general instruction samples, 2023) -- cataloged generation-quality benchmark

SafeRAG (100 test pairs across 5 sensitive domains, 2025) -- described as the first Chinese RAG security benchmark, constructed by cited work [117] with Silver Noise, inter-context conflict, soft-advertisement, and white-DoS attack types
RSB benchmark with extended EX-M / EX-L versions -- built by cited work [118] by injecting semantically similar correct-answer texts/distractors into 5 standard QA datasets including NQ and HotpotQA
quantitative_results: Survey's own literature-count statistics (Fig. 1a): 3 RAG-security papers published in 2022, 4 in 2023, 18 in 2024, 48 in 2025

Survey's own topic-share statistics (Fig. 1b) over the reviewed literature: Data Poisoning Attacks 28.4%, Defense Frameworks 14.9%, Privacy Preservation 10.4%, Membership Inference Attacks 10.4%, Reliability and Security 7.5%, Benchmarks 7.5%, Adversarial Attacks 7.5%, Data and Model Protection 6%, Cryptographic Defenses 3%, Embedding Inversion Attacks 3%, Indirect Attacks 1.5%

Cited (not survey-original) figure: poisoning attacks such as CorruptRAG and PoisonedRAG reported by citation [120] to induce predefined false/malicious answers 'with a success rate often exceeding 90%' by injecting minimal malicious samples into a large corpus

Fig. 11 (compiled from cited empirical work, not the survey's own experiment): comparison of 13 attack methods (BPRAG, WPRAG, BPI, WPI, AGGD, CRAG-AS, CRAG-AK, JamInject, JamOracle, JamOpt, AP, BadRAG, Phantom) on the NQ dataset showing suppressed Accuracy (ACC) alongside inflated Attack Success Rate (ASR) for undefended systems, and showing the TrustRAG defense outperforming the InstructRAG defense in restoring stable ACC across attack types
baselines_compared: Not applicable in the conventional sense -- the survey runs no experiments of its own. Table 1 instead compares this survey's topical coverage (RAG Architecture Anatomy, Attack Vectors & Threat Surfaces, Security Defense & Mitigation Strategies, Evaluation Benchmarks & Metrics System) against five prior RAG-security surveys cited as Wu (2025), Gu (2025), He (2025), Arz (2025), and Wang (2025), claiming this survey is the only one of the six to cover all four dimensions.
